concerns.text: The plan defines 'Graph' latency via 'In-memory graph traversal (simulating the cost)' using Python loops, while 'Relational' uses SQLite and 'Text' uses in-memory index. This creates a fundamental validity violation: the 'structural mismatch cost' is conflated with the implementation overhead of a Python simulation vs. a compiled C-engine (SQLite). The 'spike' will be an artifact of the simulation engine's inefficiency, not a property of the data structure or query complexity. The dependent variable (latency) is trivially determined by the choice of execution engine implementation, not the source type's inherent properties.

The plan uses the same DBpedia dataset for both 'Relational' (SQL joins) and 'Graph' (traversals) sources. While the data is the same, the 'Relational' engine (SQLite) and 'Graph' engine (Python simulation) are not comparable baselines. The hypothesis tests 'Source Type' (Text vs. Relational vs. Graph), but the experiment actually tests 'Engine Implementation' (Index vs. SQLite vs. Python Loop). The 'Source Type' predictor is perfectly collinear with the 'Engine Implementation' confounder, making the interaction effect uninterpretable.

The plan rejects using raw datasets because they 'fail to isolate the structural mismatch variable'. However, the proposed solution (synthetic queries on simulated engines) introduces a larger confounder: the simulation itself. If the 'Graph' engine is a naive Python traversal and the 'Relational' engine is SQLite, the 'non-linear increase' is guaranteed by the algorithmic complexity of the simulation (O(N^k) vs O(log N)), not by the 'structural mismatch' of the retrieval system. The result is true by definition of the chosen simulation algorithms, rendering the empirical validation circular.
